perturbation.original_text: Data Recipient agrees to comply with all applicable United States export laws and regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: Data Recipient agrees to comply with United States export laws and regulations to the extent that they do not conflict with Data Recipient's business interests.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a loophole by allowing the Data Recipient to selectively comply with export laws based on their own business interests. This contradicts the mandatory nature of export regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The EAR mandates strict compliance with export control regulations. Introducing a clause that allows for non-compliance based on 'business interests' directly contradicts the mandatory nature of these regulations. 15 CFR § 732.3(a) emphasizes the responsibility of exporters to determine licensing requirements and adhere to all applicable regulations, which the modified text undermines.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: EAR | Bureau of Industry and Security An official website of the United States government Here's how you know Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a significant loophole that allows the Data Recipient to potentially disregard United States export laws and regulations based on their own business interests. This directly contradicts the mandatory nature of export compliance as implied by the term 'comply' in the original text, and the law explanation, which references 15 CFR § 732.3(a), emphasizes the responsibility of exporters to adhere to all applicable regulations. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly reference the modified text, it is still relevant because it generally discusses compliance. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that the tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations. Therefore, the modified text's allowance for selective compliance directly undermines the obligatory nature of export laws, creating a clear legal contradiction and non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be fully enforceable by WECC and any entity whose Non -Public Information is not treated in accordance with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be enforceable by WECC.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the enforceability clause for entities whose Non-Public Information is mishandled weakens their legal standing. This contradicts laws that protect confidential business information and trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA provides a federal cause of action for trade secret misappropriation. By removing the right of entities whose Non-Public Information is mishandled to enforce the agreement, the contract weakens their ability to seek legal recourse under the DTSA, especially 18 U.S. Code § 1836, which allows trade secret owners to bring civil actions.

perturbation.justification: The modification removes the right of entities whose Non-Public Information is mishandled to enforce the agreement, weakening their ability to seek legal recourse under the DTSA. The original text allowed any entity whose non-public information was not treated in accordance with the agreement to enforce the agreement. The changed text removes this right. 18 U.S. Code § 1836, as shown in the scraped snippet, allows trade secret owners to bring civil actions. By removing the ability of entities to enforce the agreement when their Non-Public Information is mishandled, the contract directly undermines their ability to bring a civil action to protect their trade secrets, creating a direct contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Upon any termination of this Agreement, Data Recipient shall return to WECC all Non -Public Information in Data Recipient’s possession or destroy all Non -Public Information in Data Recipient’s possession and certify to WECC in writing that all Non -Public Information has been returned or destroyed , except as may be otherwise required by law in which case the confidentiality obligations of this Agreement shall survive termination .
perturbation.changed_text: Upon any termination of this Agreement, Data Recipient shall return to WECC all Non -Public Information in Data Recipient’s possession or destroy all Non -Public Information in Data Recipient’s possession.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause that specifies that confidentiality obligations survive termination when required by law, the agreement could be interpreted to allow the Data Recipient to disclose Non-Public Information post-termination, even if legally obligated to maintain confidentiality. This is a legal contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: The CUTSA protects trade secrets and allows for injunctive relief to prevent their misappropriation. Cal. Civ. Code § 3426.1 allows for injunctions to prevent threatened misappropriation. Removing the clause about confidentiality obligations surviving termination weakens the protection of trade secrets post-termination, directly contradicting the CUTSA's intent.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the clause that ensures confidentiality obligations survive termination when required by law. This omission contradicts the intent of CUTSA, as explained by the Law Explanation, which aims to protect trade secrets even after the termination of an agreement. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Cal. Civ. Code § 3426.1, defines 'Misappropriation' and includes disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent, particularly when knowledge of the trade secret was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy. By removing the clause, the modified text weakens the protection of trade secrets post-termination, potentially allowing the Data Recipient to disclose Non-Public Information even if legally obligated to maintain confidentiality, thus undermining the legal protections afforded to trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Data Recipient agrees to take all necessary precautions to maintain the confidentiality of the Non -Public Information and to prevent unauthorized access to it.
perturbation.changed_text: Data Recipient agrees to take reasonable precautions to maintain the confidentiality of the Non -Public Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'all necessary precautions' to 'reasonable precautions' weakens the standard of care required to protect the confidential information. This lowers the bar for liability and conflicts with laws that require a higher duty of care for sensitive data.
perturbation.law_explanation: The GLBA requires financial institutions to implement safeguards to protect the security and confidentiality of customer information. 15 U.S. Code § 6801 mandates establishing standards for information security. Reducing the required precautions from 'all necessary' to 'reasonable' potentially violates the GLBA's requirement for robust data protection measures.

Protection of nonpublic personal information (a) Privacy obligation policy It is the policy of the Congress that each financial institution has an affirmative and continuing obligation to respect the privacy of its customers and to protect the security and confidentiality of those customers' nonpublic personal information. (b) Financial institutions safeguards In furtherance
perturbation.justification: The original text states 'all necessary precautions', which creates a high standard of care. The changed text uses 'reasonable precautions', lowering the standard and potentially conflicting with the GLBA's requirement for robust data protection measures, as stated in 15 U.S. Code § 6801. The law snippet 1 states that 'each financial institution has an affirmative and continuing obligation to respect the privacy of its customers and to protect the security and confidentiality of those customers' nonpublic personal information.' By changing the language to 'reasonable', the contract weakens the protection of data, potentially failing to meet the affirmative obligation described in the law.
